title: EE: The Easy Editor
description: The Easy Editor That Actually Lives Up to Its Name
author: Lucy Ada Randall

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: FreeBSD ships with EE for a reason: it understands that most users just want to edit text, not learn a new language. In an ecosystem where every tool assumes you've read the manual, EE is a breath of fresh air.

sections.title: What Sets EE Apart
sections.content:
What sets EE apart:

  • Immediate usability: No modes, no hidden commands, no muscle memory required
  • Clear interface: Every function is visible and labeled
  • Consistent behavior: What works in one context works in all contexts

sections.title: Interface Philosophy
sections.content: Unlike vi/vim's modal approach or emacs' key chord complexity, EE presents a straightforward interface. You can see all available commands at a glance, and they do exactly what they say they'll do.

sections.title: Design Philosophy
sections.content: The philosophy behind EE is simple: software should adapt to humans, not the other way around. When you're troubleshooting a server at 2 AM, you don't want to remember which mode you're in or what obscure key combination does what.

sections.title: Power Without Complexity
sections.content: EE proves that powerful software doesn't need to be complicated. It handles all the basic editing tasks efficiently without forcing you to become a keyboard ninja.

sections.title: Usage
sections.content:
Usage is as simple as:

ee /path/to/file

No flags, no options, no configuration needed. Just open and edit.

sections.title: When to Use EE
sections.content: This is the editor I reach for when I need to make quick changes without ceremony. When the goal is editing text, not demonstrating mastery of arcane key combinations.

footer: The best interface is the one you don't have to learn.
